finding: GPT-4 vs human 85% (no ties) > human-human 81% on MT-Bench; 87%=87% on Arena.
LLM eval can mirror human ratings on story gen/adversarial but cannot fully replace; best combined.
G-Eval(GPT-4) Spearman 0.514 on SummEval, SOTA over prior metrics; still < perfect.
Length-controlled AlpacaEval lifts corr with Arena 0.93->0.98 and removes length gameability.
Panel-of-LLM-evaluators beats single GPT-4 judge, less bias, >7x cheaper.
Open 13B evaluator Pearson 0.897 with humans (45 rubrics) ~ GPT-4 0.882, >> ChatGPT 0.392.
Open evaluator narrows gap to proprietary judges on both scoring and pairwise.
LLMBar: judges good on natural set but fooled by adversarial well-formatted wrong answers.
Survey across Functionality/Methodology/Application/Meta-eval/Limitation; biases not fixed by prompt/scale.
Taxonomy what/how/benchmark to judge; calls for co-judging (human-LLM) and bias mitigation.
Comprehensive survey; pointwise/pairwise/listwise; reliability needs task-specific criteria.
TofuEval: LLM-based factual-consistency detectors (incl. GPT-4) still miss many doc-summary errors.
Fine-grained multi-dimensional summarization eval; LLM judges align better on faithfulness than coherence.
GEMBA: GPT achieves SOTA system-level correlation with MQM; needs >=GPT-3.5.
GEMBA-MQM 96.5% system-level pairwise accuracy (WMT23); reliable at SYSTEM level, NOT segment level.
LLM judges measure dialogue quality with moderate-strong human correlation; varies by dimension.
SAFE agrees with crowd 72%, wins 76% of disagreements, >20x cheaper; superhuman on a subset.
FActScore automated estimator <2% error vs human factual-precision judgments (with retrieval).
Reference-guided multi-judge improves agreement with humans on free-form QA over single judge.
RAGAS reference-free faithfulness/relevance correlate with human but unstable across pipelines.
ARES + prediction-powered inference ranks RAG systems accurately; needs small human-labeled set.
RAG-RewardBench: judges struggle to jointly reward faithfulness, relevance and citation.
CodeJudge evaluates semantic correctness without tests, beating prior LLM-eval methods.
LLM-as-judge for code gen/summarization: useful but agreement varies by language and metric.
LLM code review surfaces real issues but with many false positives; first-pass only.
Systematic OVER-correction: judges misclassify correct code as non-conformant; more prompting -> more errors.
Audit of LLM-as-judge for SE finds pervasive position/verbosity/format bias in code judgments.
LLMs systematically fail to verify code against natural-language specifications.
JudgeBench: many strong judges barely beat random on hard reasoning; math more tractable than knowledge.
Judges on math reasoning ok at final-answer checking, weaker at step/process adjudication.
Robust math LLM-judge beyond symbolic matching improves agreement with human correctness labels.
LLM relevance labels match human labellers; Cohen kappa ~ inter-human; better than third-party workers, cheaper.
Large-scale TREC study: LLM assessments largely reproduce system rankings; some divergence on borderline docs.
Perspectives paper: LLM relevance judgments promising; advocates human oversight for high-stakes IR.
ChatGPT +25pp accuracy over MTurk on relevance/stance/topic/frame; intercoder agreement > crowd & trained.
LLMs outperform expert coders and supervised classifiers at annotating political social-media messages.
27 tasks/11 datasets: accuracy median 0.85, F1 median 0.707; 8 tasks high, 9 low. MUST validate per task.
CSS: LLMs strong zero-shot on some constructs, far from human on nuanced/contextual ones.
Reliability of ChatGPT text annotation is fragile to prompt/temperature; caution against naive use.
Human-centered workflow: validate on a labeled subset, then automate; near-human label quality achievable.
Good at sentiment polarity; weaker on fine-grained aspect-based extraction (E2E-ABSA, CEE).
ChatGPT as sentiment annotation tool reaches high accuracy with cost/time advantages.
LLMs cannot fully replace humans for subjective hate-speech; still diverge even with subjectivity-aware framing.
Both human and LLM hate-speech annotations carry socio-demographic biases; targets/annotators matter.
LLMs better on explicit than implicit/coded hate; vulnerable, relabeling agreement poor.
SMEs agree with LLM judges only 68% (dietetics)/64% (mental health); LLM judges unreliable for expert tasks.
PRISMA-ScR (49 studies): 75.5% benchmarking, 85.7% pointwise; risk-of-bias absent in 73.5%; needs governance.
ICC 0.818 overall; structured docs 0.82-0.93, semi-structured 0.64-0.79, complex reasoning 0.47-0.71.
LLMEval-Med: real-world clinical benchmark with physician validation; agreement varies by criterion.
Radiologists score GPT-4 impressions lower than human ones; some referring physicians prefer GPT-4.
GPT-4 differential-diagnosis accuracy 93-95% from reports, no significant difference vs radiologists.
HealthBench: 48,562 clinician criteria; model grader shows high concordance with physician ratings.
Automatic similarity and LLM-as-judge disagree on clinical dialogue; neither alone reliable.
French medical open-ended QA: judge reliability degrades with language + clinical specificity.
Reliable for cognitive attributes (guidance, safety, interpretation), unreliable for empathy/affect; inflated scores.
CounselBench: expert evaluation + adversarial probes expose unreliable LLM judging in counseling.
Multi-attribute human eval: LLM support judged structured/safe but emotionally misattuned.
LeMAJ: decomposing answers into Legal Data Points raises correlation with expert lawyers and IAA.
Legal doc recommendation: GPT-4o Spearman 0.73, Gwet AC2 0.78 vs expert rankings.
TRIDENT safety benchmark (finance/medicine/law): strict unanimous expert gold; judges partial.
Business-driven financial benchmark; expert-annotated; LLM-judge alignment moderate, capability-bound.
Automated essay scoring with GPT reaches QWK ~0.67-0.80 across settings; near human agreement.
GPT-4 best QWK 0.57 < human baseline 0.66; alone insufficient for AES.
K-12 short-answer marking: GPT-4 few-shot kappa 0.70 close to human 0.75.
ASAG: GPT-4 consistent but slight agreement and systematic over-scoring; needs human moderation.
Multi-dimensional writing assessment: reliable on some traits, weaker on style/voice.
Fine-tuned/few-shot GPT-4.1 matches human experts evaluating tutor feedback quality.
Human validation of multi-LLM evaluation of K-12 science materials shows usable agreement.
Pedagogical quality of AI vs human formative feedback: experts and AI diverge on nuanced pedagogy.
LLM creativity ratings near-zero correlation with expert TTCW judgments; experts judge differently.
LitBench: off-the-shelf Claude-3.7 73% agreement; trained reward models 78% with human preferences.
Non-experts and LLMs rate by linguistic complexity; only experts capture semantic creativity.
LLMs as creative-writing evaluators: moderate alignment, struggle with originality/voice.
GPT-4 review feedback overlaps human points ~ as much as two humans overlap; useful but shallow.
ChatGPT complete agreement with human reviewer comments only 6.7%; limited emulation of reviewers.
GPT-4 vs humans appraising case-report quality: 41.9-80.9% agreement across items (Murad tool).
GPT-4 as added reviewer in PRISMA SLR abstract screening: near-perfect agreement (Cohen kappa >0.9).
MLLM-as-a-Judge: pairwise 79.3% human agreement but scoring/ranking much weaker; many biases.
Open VLM evaluator correlates with humans on fine-grained image-response scoring.
WildVision Arena: VLM judges approximate human preference rankings on real multimodal chats.
Multimodal RewardBench: even top VLMs reach only 72% accuracy vs human preference.
VQAScore beats CLIPScore/PickScore/HPSv2/ImageReward at matching human T2I alignment ratings.
Human Preference Score v2 predicts human T2I preferences; trained on large human-rating corpus.
Judges inconsistent across languages; low-resource much worse; scale/multilingual training don't fix it.
MM-Eval multilingual meta-eval: judges drop on non-English, esp. low-resource and linguistics subsets.
Empirical study: multilingual LLM judges remain unreliable; ensembles/fine-tuning partially help.
RewardBench: chosen/rejected accuracy varies widely; safety/reasoning hardest; weak link to downstream.
IF-RewardBench: best judge Kendall 0.609 << human 0.755 ranking instruction-following responses.
LLMs as political-truth annotators: looser than humans on vague claims; mixed reliability.
Multilingual fact-checking: facts harder than opinions; small models overconfident & inaccurate.
LLM claim-matching comparable to human ratings; fine-tuned rivals large pre-trained.
IFEval: verifiable constraints enable objective auto-eval WITHOUT an LLM judge (no judge bias).
Fine-grained instruction-following critic improves per-requirement agreement with humans.
Human-GPT-4 Fleiss kappa ~0.46 (moderate); GPT-4 self-consistency ~0.87 (>human); hybrid advised.
QualiGPT supports qualitative coding; agreement adequate for deductive codebooks, weaker inductively.
Thematic analysis case study: GPT-4 accelerates coding with reasonable but imperfect theme overlap.
LLM pre-annotation + human refinement improves NER datasets cost-effectively (hybrid).
LLM explanations are good proxies for human explanations to recover NLI label distributions.
Collaborative annotation: verify LLM labels selectively to reach high quality at lower human cost.
LLMs moderately agree with experts on argument quality and show HIGHER IAA than humans, but gender bias.
LLMs favor own generations even when humans rate them equal; self-recognition correlates with self-preference.
LLM evaluators are inconsistent and biased; sensitive to trivial prompt changes.
CoBBLEr: ~40% of comparisons biased; Rank-Biased Overlap with humans only 44%.
Both human AND LLM judges biased & attackable; cutting-edge judges still carry considerable bias.
Judges reward style/completeness; correctness & safety are weak predictors of their scores.
Short universal adversarial phrases inflate judge scores; absolute scoring far less robust than comparative.
EvalGen: aligning LLM evaluators needs human grading; 'criteria drift' makes fixed rubrics insufficient.
Temperature strongly governs judge consistency; low temp near-deterministic, high temp error surges.
CALM framework quantifies 12 judge biases; even best judges show non-trivial attack success.
alt-test: leave-one-out statistical test for justified replacement; LLMs sometimes pass, closed-source best.
JUDGE-BENCH (20 tasks): GPT-4o categorical kappa 0.28+-0.32, graded rho 0.50+-0.21; NOT ready to replace humans.
Judge's Verdict: 27/54 judges reach Tier-1; some judge MORE consistently than typical humans (Turing test for judges).
Guidance on IAA metric choice; subjective tasks (e.g., sarcasm) human alpha often <0.35 (low human ceiling).
ChatGPT as NLG evaluator correlates with humans on some tasks but trails fine-tuned metrics; preliminary.
LLMs are NOT yet human-level evaluators for abstractive summarization; unstable, sensitive to setup.
INSTRUCTSCORE: explainable fine-tuned evaluator with diagnostic feedback; strong human correlation.
Instruction-tuned LLM news summaries rated comparable to freelance-writer summaries by humans.
LLM medical-evidence summaries show factual errors/hallucination on expert review; not yet reliable.
GPT-4 shows high intra-rater consistency rating student feedback across repeated runs (reliable rater).
LLM-based automatic short-answer grading usable in undergraduate medical education with oversight.
GPT-4 rates short L2 essays on CEFR scale; few-shot approaches but stays below expert agreement.
Comparison of human vs LLM exam grading: correlated but systematic divergences on nuanced answers.
AI grading of intro-physics solutions feasible but struggles with partial credit; needs oversight.
AlpacaFarm: simulated LLM-judge win-rates rank methods at ~0.98 correlation with human win-rates, 50x cheaper.
ChatGPT essay scoring in dental exams: only moderate reliability vs examiners; not stand-alone.
Survey of LLM-based NLG evaluation: status, meta-evaluation methods, and open reliability challenges.
GPTAraEval: ChatGPT lags dedicated/Arabic models across 44 Arabic NLP tasks (multilingual gap).
ChatGPT/Bard assessment-item alignment shows only low-moderate reliability vs human standards.
Fine-grained relevance scoring (beyond yes/no) improves zero-shot LLM ranker agreement with humans.
LLM relevance labellers can be fooled by injected keywords to mark irrelevant docs relevant (robustness).
Empirical study of LLM-as-a-judge in software testing: partial substitution, notable disagreement cases.
'Mind the Hype': cautions that ChatGPT annotation reliability is overstated; reproducibility/validity risks.
ChatGPT paraphrases for intent classification: more diverse but less robust than crowdsourced ones.
FineSurE: fine-grained faithfulness/completeness/conciseness scoring correlates better with humans.
'HOT' ChatGPT detects hateful/offensive/toxic content with fair human agreement but inconsistencies remain.
GPT-4 validated for structured clinical-event classification with high agreement vs clinicians.
Using LLMs as judges for clinical AI summaries: partial alignment; can miss clinically important errors.
Human-centered design recommendations for deploying LLM-as-a-judge responsibly (with human oversight).
Scoping review of LLMs for generative mental-health tasks: validation and safety evaluation gaps remain.
Human-like summarization evaluation with ChatGPT; correlates with humans but sensitive to aspect/prompt.
ChatGPT as a factual-inconsistency evaluator for summarization; decent but below specialized metrics on some sets.
CoAnnotating allocates items between human and LLM by uncertainty, preserving quality at lower cost.
Compares human- vs LLM-generated annotations; LLM labels competitive on several text tasks.
Comprehensive ChatGPT-vs-human annotation analysis; strong on objective categories, weaker on subjective.
LLM entity-level sentiment annotation approaches but trails expert annotators.
Language-model ensembles label free-text data near human quality with calibrated abstention.
LLM emotion annotation moderately agrees with humans; struggles with ambiguous/affective labels.
CLARIN-Emo: ChatGPT emotion annotation usable, but human annotation remains superior for model training.
Comparative analysis of LLM stance annotation in social media; competitive with crowd on clear cases.
Human vs ChatGPT annotation for crisis misinformation; LLM helps but interpretable human labels still differ.
ChatGPT supports content analysis with reasonable concordance; recommended as assistant, not replacement.
Humans vs ChatGPT on financial-corpus annotation; ChatGPT trails experts on nuanced financial labels.
Argues LLM relevance assessment STILL cannot replace human assessors for reliable IR evaluation (counterpoint).
Benchmarks LLM relevance-judgment methods; agreement varies widely by method and prompt design.
Error-analysis prompting yields human-like MT evaluation, improving correlation with MQM labels.
ChatGPT rates NL-explanation quality like humans, but agreement depends strongly on the rating scale.
HC3: experts compare ChatGPT vs human answers; gaps remain on helpfulness and factual depth.
Automatic evaluation methods for instruction-tuned LLMs are only moderately reliable; method choice matters.
Networks of collaborating/debating LLM evaluators are fairer and better-aligned than single judges.
LLM evaluators exhibit nationality bias; overall quality good but not bias-free.
Quantifies verbosity bias in LLM preference labeling: longer answers favored independent of quality.
Split-and-Merge aligns position biases in LLM evaluators, improving agreement with humans.
Pairwise preference aligns LLM evaluators with human judgement better than pointwise scoring.
Survey/guideline for evaluating LLM alignment dimensions; evaluation reliability among the key challenges.
Evaluates bias and behavioral diversity in LLM annotation; systematic biases arise from design choices.
VeriFact verifies facts in LLM clinical text against the EHR; agreement with clinicians moderate-to-high.
LLMs emulate human-expert evaluation with limited accuracy on nuanced health-communication judgments.
Automating evaluation of healthcare AI text with an LLM judge; partial alignment, residual clinical error.
Automated multimodal assessment of classroom encouragement/warmth correlates moderately with human raters.
Reliability study of GPT for language assessment; promising but requires human moderation.
Asks whether LLM creativity has peaked; human evaluation shows a plateau and divergence from human creativity.
ChatGPT risk-of-bias assessment in RCTs partially agrees with human reviewers; not yet standalone.
Judging the Judges: LLM judges align moderately with humans but are vulnerable and inconsistent across setups.
GPT-4 is an effective zero-shot evaluator for scientific figure captions, correlating with expert ratings.
Multidimensional ChatGPT evaluation of text style transfer correlates with humans on some dimensions.
Beyond Agreement: AES methods can match scores while misaligning on rationale -- agreement alone misleads.
Self-preference bias: LLM judges favour outputs they assign lower perplexity, i.e., their own style.
LLM relevance assessors agree with EACH OTHER more than with humans -- shared blind spots, not human alignment.
Human-AI analysis of prompt sensitivity in LLM relevance judgments: small prompt changes shift agreement.
WildBench: checklist-guided LLM-judge scores correlate ~0.98 with human Chatbot-Arena Elo on real-user tasks.
Is an LLM a useful evaluator for clinical note generation? Partially; misses some clinically salient errors.
Evaluation of LLMs in qualitative research: useful coding support with moderate agreement; human oversight needed.
Large-scale Moral Machine on LLMs: LLM moral judgments partly track human preferences but deviate systematically.
Practical pipeline for training an LLM-as-a-judge; fine-tuning improves human alignment over prompting alone.
LLMs-in-the-loop: LLM annotations drive active learning, cutting human labeling while retaining accuracy.
PEDANTS: interpretable answer-equivalence judging for QA matches human judgments far better than exact match.
Comparative analysis of ChatGPT vs human peer review: overlap on broad points, divergence on technical depth.
JudgeLM: fine-tuned judges reach >90% agreement with GPT-4, exceeding human-human agreement; swap/reference augmentation.
PandaLM judge LLM validated on a human-labeled test set; PandaLM-7B ~ GPT-3.5/4, 70B surpasses them.
Auto-J generative judge with critiques across 58 real-world scenarios; pairwise + pointwise, human-aligned but imperfect.
Shepherd critic (7B) produces feedback rated equivalent/preferred to ChatGPT; 53-87% win-rate vs alternatives.
CritiqueLLM critique model correlates with humans on most tasks but degrades on reasoning/math critiques.
Self-Rewarding LMs use LLM-as-judge to reward themselves; reward quality improves over iterations (but self-bias risk).
Meta-Rewarding: the model judges its own judgments; meta-judge step improves judging skill and alignment.
Arena-Hard/BenchBuilder: automatic LLM-judge benchmark reaches 89.1% agreement (up to 98.6% confidence) with Chatbot Arena.
Chatbot Arena: open human-preference platform; crowd votes agree with experts and ground LLM-judge validation (>2.5M votes).
CHALET: human-LLM partnership for theory-driven qualitative analysis via iterative coding and disagreement analysis (HCI).
LLMs as workers in human-computation pipelines: replicate multi-step crowdsourcing workflows with mixed fidelity (CSCW/HCOMP).
Can ChatGPT reproduce human labels for social-computing tasks? Good on some, poor on subjective/cultural ones (CSCW).
EvaluLLM: interactive HCI system for LLM-assisted evaluation of generative outputs against user-defined criteria (IUI).
MEGAnno+: human-LLM collaborative annotation with LLM pre-labels and required human verification.
LLM feedback on UI mockups partially matches expert designer feedback; useful first-pass but misses design nuance (HCI).
LLM-Eval: single-prompt multi-dimensional dialogue evaluation correlating with human ratings across benchmarks.
ChatEval: multi-agent debate among LLM evaluators improves agreement with human judgments over a single judge.
LLM-Rubric: multidimensional rubric scoring with a small calibration network markedly improves human correlation.
ChatGPT vs crowdsourcing vs experts annotating open-domain dialogue: ChatGPT between crowd and expert quality.
The Next Chapter: human + automatic evaluation of LLM storytelling; LLM evaluators only partially track human judgments.
AFaCTA: LLM-assisted annotation of factual-claim detection with calibrated confidence; high agreement with experts.
RLAIF vs RLHF: AI (LLM) preference labels yield comparable downstream gains to human labels on summarization/dialogue.
Trust-or-Escalate: confidence-based selective LLM judging with provable human-agreement guarantees; escalate hard cases.
Preference Leakage: when judge and generated-data model are related, judges are biased toward those outputs (contamination).
Evaluating the Evaluator: LLM judges often fail to adhere to the task evaluation instructions/rubric they are given.
SedarEval: self-adaptive scoring rubrics improve LLM-judge agreement with human grading over generic prompts.
Promises and pitfalls of LLM dataset annotations: convenient but can inject systematic artifacts vs human labels.
Re-evaluating automatic LLM system ranking: judge-induced rankings only partially align with human-preference rankings.
Empirical study: LLM-as-judge design choices (prompt, scale, format) materially change human agreement.
Empirical study of LLM-as-a-judge for LLM evaluation: fine-tuning and aggregation help, but biases persist.
Codebook+GPT-3 deductive coding reaches fair-to-substantial agreement with expert coders; reliability hinges on codebook design.
Inductive+deductive coding with AI: Fleiss kappa ~0.46 (deductive), 0.57 (inductive) vs human coders; moderate, hybrid advised.
CollabCoder: lower-barrier rigorous workflow embedding LLM suggestions in collaborative inductive coding; humans keep interpretive authority.
Chain-of-thought coding matches human performance on 8/9 hermeneutic tasks above substantial-kappa; scalable but task-bounded.
ChatQDA on-device open-source coding: usable but users show 'conditional trust', doubting interpretive nuance and consistency.
Inductive thematic analysis of interviews with ChatGPT is feasible and plausible, but themes need human verification and lack depth.
LLM-in-the-loop thematic analysis: human-AI collaboration yields themes comparable to human analysts with large efficiency gains.
ChatGPT as a thematic-analysis research assistant: helpful for breadth, but reflexive interpretation remains human work.
Multi-LLM thematic analysis with dual reliability (kappa + semantic): Gemini kappa 0.907, GPT-4o 0.853, Claude 0.842 vs humans.
LLM thematic summarization in qualitative health research: 79.7% agreement with human interpretation (substantial).
DeTAILS: iterative LLM-supported deep thematic analysis keeps the researcher in control across coding and theme development.
Human vs generative-AI thematic analysis: comparable high-level themes, large efficiency gain, but AI misses nuanced sub-themes.
LLM-assisted content analysis: LLMs can apply codebooks with substantial agreement; recommend human validation on a labeled subset.
Scaling hermeneutics: guide to reflexive LLM content analysis preserving interpretive validity at scale.
ChatGPT supports content analysis with reasonable concordance; recommended as assistant, not replacement.
Interviews with qualitative researchers reveal uses, tensions, and intentions: LLMs valued for breadth but threaten interpretive rigor/positionality.
Ethics of generative-AI qualitative data analysis: warns that uncritical use erodes interpretive accountability and rigor.
Comparing ChatGPT and a human qualitative researcher: useful for surface coding, 'imperfections' in depth and context.
GPT-4 vs human researchers on health-care qualitative data: moderate agreement (kappa ~0.40); AI aids but does not replace.
Redefining qualitative analysis in the AI era: ChatGPT accelerates coding; calls for new human-AI qualitative workflows.
GPT-4 supports analysis of textual data in tasks requiring highly skilled (legal) coding; promising but below expert reliability.
LLMs analysing classroom dialogue: moderate agreement with human coders; useful for scale, weaker on pedagogical subtlety.
Human-GPT-4 Fleiss kappa ~0.46; GPT-4 self-consistency ~0.87 (> human); hybrid coding advised.
QualiGPT tool: adequate agreement for deductive codebooks, weaker inductively; positioned as assistant.
CHALET: human-LLM partnership for theory-driven qualitative analysis via iterative coding and disagreement analysis.
Evaluation of LLMs in qualitative research: moderate agreement; human oversight needed for valid interpretation.
Thematic analysis case study: GPT-4 accelerates coding with reasonable but imperfect theme overlap.
'Out of one, many': LLMs encode enough sociodemographic structure to simulate human survey samples ('silicon sampling') at group level.
LLMs replacing participants misportray and FLATTEN demographic groups; cannot capture positionality (3,200 humans, 16 identities).
Can generative AI improve social science? Promising for annotation/simulation but validity and bias risks demand human benchmarking.
Perils of synthetic survey data: LLM 'respondents' distort variances/correlations vs real humans; unsafe as a replacement.
Uncertainty-quantification view: an LLM is worth only a bounded number of human respondents; substitution adds hidden uncertainty.
CoMPosT: LLM simulations of human subgroups are often caricatures, especially for marginalized identities.
Critical analysis of synthetic users in HCI: cannot capture unpredictable behavior; should not stand in for real users.
'Let the LLMs talk': simulating users in conversational search; useful for evaluation but diverges from real user behavior.
LLM-generated personas can be plausible and useful for ideation but risk stereotyping and lack grounded data.
PersonaCraft: data-grounded LLM personas from survey segments; better grounded but still need human validation.
LLM synthetic personas encode gender/role bias from a participatory-design lens; unsafe as stand-ins for real users.
GPT-4o heuristic evaluation overlaps only 21.2% with expert-found issues and hallucinates false positives; not a substitute.
Synthetic heuristic evaluation finds 73-77% of issues, exceeding 5 human evaluators (57-63%) but with different error profile.
UICrit dataset shows LLM UI critique falls short of expert designers; few-shot/visual prompting gives +55% but gap remains.
Visual prompting with iterative refinement improves LLM design-critique quality toward, but not to, human-expert level.
ChatGPT explored as designer/user/evaluator in conversational design; useful provocation, not a reliable evaluator.
LLM feedback on UI mockups partially matches expert designer feedback; useful first pass, misses design nuance.
ChatGPT +25pp accuracy over MTurk on relevance/stance/topic/frame; intercoder agreement exceeds crowd and trained coders.
ChatGPT-4 outperforms experts and crowd workers at annotating political social-media messages.
'Beginning of an end of manual annotation?': ChatGPT competitive on automatic genre/text annotation but not uniformly.
CoAnnotating allocates items between human and LLM by uncertainty, preserving quality at lower human cost.
LLMs as workers in human-computation pipelines: replicate multi-step crowdsourcing workflows with mixed fidelity.
Can ChatGPT reproduce human labels for social-computing tasks? Good on some, poor on subjective/cultural ones.
ChatGPT vs crowdsourcing vs experts annotating open-domain dialogue: ChatGPT between crowd and expert quality.
Human-algorithmic interaction with an LLM for annotation: human-AI workflow improves throughput with oversight.
LLMs as research tools across HCI data work: maps applications and evaluation gaps; calls for validation norms.
A primer on using ChatGPT for HCI research: opportunities across the method pipeline with caution on validity.
LLM assistance homogenizes human creative ideation (less collective diversity); a caution for LLM-mediated design research.
